seo.title: RabbitMQ Message Tracing — Track Messages | Qarote
seo.description: Trace individual RabbitMQ messages from publish to delivery or dead-letter. Debug routing misconfigurations, schema mismatches, and message loss without raw log parsing.

hero.title: Follow every message through your RabbitMQ topology.
hero.subtitle: Qarote's message tracing captures the full journey of individual messages — from exchange routing to queue delivery or dead-lettering. Debug routing misconfigurations, schema mismatches, and unexpected redeliveries without digging through raw logs.

traceTypes.heading: Every step of the message journey

items.title: Publish path & exchange routing
items.description: See exactly which exchange received a message and how it was routed. Confirm that binding keys match the published routing key, and surface mismatches before they result in silent message loss.

items.title: Queue delivery confirmation
items.description: Verify that a message reached its intended queue and was acknowledged by a consumer. Distinguish between messages that were delivered but nacked versus messages that never arrived at the queue at all.

items.title: Dead-letter routing
items.description: Track messages that were rejected, expired, or exceeded their max-length limit into dead-letter exchanges. See the full path from the originating queue to the DLX and into the DLQ.

items.title: Message redelivery & nack events
items.description: Identify messages that are being repeatedly redelivered. A consumer that nacks and requeues a message without fixing the underlying issue produces an infinite redelivery loop that is easy to miss without tracing.

items.title: Routing key mismatches
items.description: Surface messages published with a routing key that matches no binding on the target exchange. These messages are silently dropped by RabbitMQ — tracing is the only way to confirm they were published at all.

items.title: Unroutable messages
items.description: Catch messages that arrive at a fanout or topic exchange but are discarded because no queue is bound. Critical for detecting topology gaps after queue bindings are changed or consumers are redeployed.

steps.title: Enable tracing on a vhost or exchange
steps.description: Qarote activates the RabbitMQ firehose on the target vhost or exchange with a single click. No manual plugin configuration or CLI access to your broker nodes is required.

steps.title: Messages captured via firehose
steps.description: RabbitMQ publishes a copy of every matching message to the amq.rabbitmq.trace exchange. Qarote consumes these trace events and stores the routing metadata, headers, and payload summary.

steps.title: Inspect the full trace in the dashboard
steps.description: Browse and filter captured traces by exchange, queue, routing key, or time window. Click any trace event to see its complete journey — publish path, delivery outcome, and any dead-letter hops.

whyTracing.heading: Silent message loss is the hardest bug to reproduce
whyTracing.p1: When a message disappears in a RabbitMQ topology, there is no exception, no log line, and no error response. The publisher got a confirm, the broker accepted the message, and then nothing. Reconstructing what happened means reading broker logs, checking binding configurations, and reasoning about topology state that may have already changed — all without a direct record of the event.
whyTracing.p2: Routing bugs are particularly hard to catch in development because they only appear under specific topology conditions: a binding that looked correct in staging but was missing a wildcard segment, a header exchange that required an exact match the producer was not setting, or a dead-letter exchange that was configured but never bound to a queue. Message tracing gives you a first-party record of every routing decision the broker made.
whyTracing.p3: The RabbitMQ management plugin includes a basic tracing facility (`rabbitmq_tracing`), but it writes raw log files to disk on each broker node and requires SSH access to retrieve. Qarote surfaces the same firehose data through a searchable, filterable dashboard — no log parsing, no broker node access, and no impact on your existing monitoring setup.

items.question: Does enabling message tracing affect broker performance?
items.answer: Yes — the RabbitMQ firehose publishes a copy of every traced message to the amq.rabbitmq.trace exchange, which adds overhead proportional to message throughput. Qarote is designed to be enabled selectively: target a specific vhost or exchange, capture what you need, and disable tracing when the investigation is complete. Do not leave tracing permanently enabled on high-throughput exchanges in production.

items.question: Which Qarote plan includes message tracing?
items.answer: Message tracing requires a Developer or Enterprise license. It is not available in the free open-source MIT edition. A Developer license ($348/yr) covers a single workspace; Enterprise ($1,188/yr) covers unlimited workspaces and includes priority support.

items.question: Does message tracing require the rabbitmq_tracing plugin?
items.answer: No. Qarote uses the RabbitMQ firehose mechanism (the `amq.rabbitmq.trace` exchange), not the `rabbitmq_tracing` plugin. The firehose is part of the RabbitMQ core and does not need to be separately enabled. Qarote handles activation on your behalf when you start a trace session.

items.question: Can I filter traces by queue or exchange?
items.answer: Yes. The trace dashboard lets you filter by exchange, queue, routing key, and time range. You can scope a trace session to a specific vhost or to a named exchange to reduce overhead and narrow results to the messages you care about.

items.question: Does message tracing work in self-hosted deployments?
items.answer: Yes. Qarote's message tracing works in any deployment mode — binary, Docker Compose, or Dokku. The only requirement is that the Qarote process can reach the RabbitMQ management API and has credentials with sufficient permissions to consume from the amq.rabbitmq.trace exchange.
